subject: SOCDS 2025/26 Update Week #5 - PreK Orange
text:
Email Ms. Annemarie
Getting Ready to Celebrate
America's 250th Year
As our country prepares to celebrate the 250th anniversary of its founding next July, we want to provide children with many opportunities to think about what it means to be an American.
Because the children are already familiar with maps and flags as symbols for holidays and cultural recognitions, we will build on this knowledge by exploring American maps from both the past and the present. We will use age-appropriate language, but we will not shy away from discussing difficult truths—much like the way we talk about the murder of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.
These conversations will begin this fall and continue through July 2026, giving us time to engage with a variety of ideas and reflect on the promise of 
America.
To start, we will learn Woody Guthrie’s song
This Land Is Your Land
. This work will likely take us several months, and it will connect beautifully to our map explorations while also helping us expand vocabulary and deepen our sense of shared belonging.
If you have any thoughts, ideas, or concerns, please share them with us. This is a journey we are taking together, and we deeply value your family’s experiences, insights, and even worries along the way.
This Land Is Your Land
This land is your land, and this land is my land
From California to the New York Island
From the Redwood Forest to the Gulf Stream Waters
This land was made for you and me
As I went walking that ribbon of highway
And I saw above me that endless skyway
I saw below me that golden valley
This land was made for you and me
I roamed and rambled and I followed my footsteps
To the sparkling sands of her diamond deserts
All around me a voice was a-sounding
This land was made for you and me
There was a big high wall there that tried to stop me
Sign was painted, said, "Private Property"
But on the back side, it didn't say nothing
This land was made for you and me
When the sun comes shining, then I was strolling
And the wheat fields waving and the dust clouds rolling
A voice come chanting as the fog was lifting
This land was made for you and me
This land is your land, and this land is my land
From California to the New York Island
From Redwood Forest to the Gulf Stream Waters
This land was made for you and me
Gardening with Ms. Linda
The Fall season is the perfect time to begin our study of trees. We discussed the fact that trees are plants. They are among the largest and longest living plants on Earth. We talked about the many ways we depend on trees to give us food and other necessities such as paper and building materials. We learned that trees prevent soil erosion, produce oxygen, absorb carbon dioxide and provide habitats for countless species. Most importantly, trees give us shade on a hot day. We were amazed to discover that a tiny acorn could grow up to be as big as the mighty oak tree in the front yard of our school!
The Kindergarteners tasted the pickles they made last week and discussed the importance of knowing how to preserve the fruits and vegetables we harvest.
Monday, October 13th SOCDS Closed
MEND Food and Personal Item Drive October 14th-18th
Our annual drive to support
MEND (Meeting Essential Needs with Dignity)
will take place
Tuesday, October 14th through Friday, October 17th
. On Friday, the Green Bean Van will visit SOCDS, and the children will have a chance to see firsthand how their thoughtful donations help community members in need. A big thank-you to PEP for helping us organize this important event.
This year, MEND has asked especially for
shelf-stable milk
and
cooking oil
. As always,
non-perishable, non-expired food items
are welcome. In addition,
unopened personal care items
such as 
toothbrushes, toothpaste, and soap are much needed.
Thank you in advance for your generosity and kindness as we help MEND prepare for the long winter months ahead.
If you have any questions or concerns 
please text (1-646-226-8790) or email learn@socds.com.
Annemarie
Art with Ms. Jenny
This week the children explored the art of
Peter Anton
, a contemporary American artist best known for his giant, realistic sculptures of chocolates, candies, and—of course—donuts.
Inspired by his work, Ms. Jenny invited the children to experiment with clay, rolling, pinching, and sculpting their own creations. Thanks to the warm weather, the pieces dried quickly and were soon ready for the final touch—bright, cheerful paint colors.

Logistics
Dress Appropriately for the Weather:
Please dress your child in appropriate gear for the day.  There is no bad weather just poor clothing.
Morning Snack
:  Please send in a morning, and if your child is staying for enrichment please send in an afternoon snack as well.  We generally allow children to choose their food and to listen to their bodies, but if you have any particular preference for what they eat and when, please just label the containers and we will accommodate.  Please remember we are a NUT FREE environment.
Water Bottles
:  Please send in a labeled water bottle with your child each day.  We do make sure that they drink water 
throughout the day.  We have two water fountains which we use to refill the bottles (so they may come home full.)
Parking:
Please remember that Vose Avenue is an active busy street.  Please pull up on the
school side
of Vose in front of the school.  It is safest to go around the block instead of turning around in driveways.  For your child's safety please try to have them leave your car from the sidewalk side of the car.
Please do not block our neighbors driveway, even for a few minutes.
Tuition:
As I receive payments I update the statement and send you a copy.  If you do not receive those from me please let me know.
Hours
Morning Care 7:30-8:30am
Morning Drop off is between 8:30-9am
Afternoon Pick Up is between 3:10- 3:20pm
Extended Day 3:20-5:30/5:45pm
South Orange Country Day School
